tasks.prompt: We run a SaaS app with about 35,000 customers in Qdrant. Today each customer has its own collection, and we're hitting operational limits with many tiny tenants plus a few very large ones. How should we redesign this so tenant queries stay isolated and performance doesn't collapse?

rubric.text: Says to abandon collection-per-tenant (doesn't scale past a few hundred; ~1000 collection limit) and move to a single shared collection partitioned by a tenant-id payload key
Recommends a payload index on the tenant field with is_tenant=true for partitioning/isolation and read performance
Addresses the uneven tenant sizes with tiered multitenancy: promote the few very large tenants to dedicated shards while keeping the many small tenants on shared shards
For ~35k tenants on a multi-node cluster, recommends custom sharding (assign tenants to shards by tenant-id hash) to localize each tenant's queries to specific nodes instead of broadcasting to all
Uses payload_m to build per-tenant HNSW subgraphs instead of a global HNSW index for the multi-tenant collection
Notes true per-tenant isolation via separate collections is only for compliance/encryption needs - the exception, not the default
Keeps or recommends collection-per-tenant as the scalable design (without a compliance reason)
Omits is_tenant=true on the tenant index (kills sequential-read performance) or builds a global HNSW for the multi-tenant collection

tasks.prompt: I have a question about payload indexes in Qdrant multitenancy. Suppose tenants share the same collection: Tenant A inserts: { "field1": 42 }. Tenant B inserts: { "field1": "books" }. If field1 is indexed as keyword, what happens to Tenant A's integer values? Are payload index schemas shared globally across the collection even in multitenant mode, or isolated per tenant? What's the recommended way to handle tenant-specific payload schemas?

rubric.text: States that data is isolated per shard and point ID, so inserts using different point IDs coexist
Explains that in the default scenario (without custom sharding), if the same point ID is used with different data, the last applied operation wins

Notes that with custom sharding, inserting the same point ID with different data into two different shards is undefined behavior, and the user is responsible for not duplicating point IDs across shards
